text:
Tensors
Tensors are basic data structures in PyTorch that support vecotrized computations, GPU acceleration, and automatic differentiation.

Generation


Code
X = [[0,1,2], [3,4,5]] # list
Xt = torch.tensor(X)   # tensor

print(f'type(X): {type(X)}')
print(f'type(Xt): {type(Xt)}')

#help(torch.tensor) # first aid


type(X): <class 'list'>
type(Xt): <class 'torch.Tensor'>



Create from numpy
numpy array refer to contiguous data buffers in memory. This allows for efficient data sharing with torch tensors.


Code
import numpy as np
import torch

Xn = np.array( [0,1,2] )    # numpy array
Xc = torch.tensor(Xn)       # tensor with **copy** of data
Xs = torch.from_numpy(Xn)   # tensor with **shared** data

# different Python objects (wrappers to data) have different addresses
print("Xn id:", id(Xn)) 
print("Xs id:", id(Xs))
print("Xc id:", id(Xc))

# data buffers address: points to first element of contiguous data
print("Xn address:", Xn.ctypes.data) 
print("Xs address:", Xs.data_ptr()) 
print("Xc address:", Xc.data_ptr()) 

# Example
Xn[1]=42
print("Xn: ", Xn) 
print("Xc:", Xc) 
print("Xs:", Xs)

PyTorch objects are associated with a device. If possible use GPU for speed.

def detect_device():
    import torch
    if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
        # Mac Metal
        return "mps"
    elif torch.cuda.is_available():
        # NVIDIA
        return "cuda"
    else:
        return "cpu"

device = detect_device()
print('device: ', device)

title: Pytorch Starter
text:
Computational Graph and Autograd
\[
L = x \cdot (a + 2b) \quad \text{with} \quad (a=2, b=3, x=1)
\]


Code
# Lead nodes; inputs to the graph
a = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.tensor(3.0, requires_grad=True)
x = torch.tensor(1.0, requires_grad=True)

# operations in computational graph: nodes z and L
z = a + 2*b
L = x*z  # L = x*(a+2b);  dL/dx = z; dL/dz = x

# endows also inner nodes (z and L) with gradient functions
print(f'z_grad = {z.grad_fn}')
print(f'L_grad = {L.grad_fn}')


z_grad = <AddBackward0 object at 0x3019212a0>
L_grad = <MulBackward0 object at 0x300f66350>




Code
a.grad = b.grad = x.grad = None  # clear old grads if any
z.retain_grad() # per default pytorch keeps gradients only for leave nodes
L.backward()    # fill gradients with respect to L

print('x.grad:', x.grad) # dL/dx = z  = a + 2*b = 5
print('a.grad:', a.grad) # dL/da = (dL/dz) (dz/da) = x * 1
print('b.grad:', b.grad) # dL/db = (dL/dz) (dz/db) = x * 2
print('z.grad:', z.grad) # dL/dz = x


x.grad: tensor(8.)
a.grad: tensor(1.)
b.grad: tensor(2.)
z.grad: tensor(1.)

title: Pytorch Starter
text:
Building Larger Models (later)
We can define larger models from small building blocks. Ultimately this will help to abstract from individual layers, their initialization, normalization, dropouts etc.


Code
import torch.nn as nn

class MyModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_in, n_out):
        # inherit stuff from nn.Module
        super().__init__()
        # define layers: just some random examples
        self.linear = nn.Linear(n_in, n_out)
        self.norm = nn.Dropout(p=0.3)

    # connect layers defined above --> forward pass
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.linear(x)
        x = self.norm(x)
        x = nn.functional.sigmoid(x)
        return x

model = MyModel(10, 1)
print(model)


MyModel(
  (linear): Linear(in_features=10, out_features=1, bias=True)
  (norm): Dropout(p=0.3, inplace=False)
)



Optimizer and Loss Function


Code
import torch.optim as optim

learning_rate = 0.01 # hyperparameter

optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr = learning_rate)

loss_function = nn.MSELoss()




model.train() vs. model.eval()
two different modes for training and evaluation








Feature
model.train() (Training Mode)
model.eval() (Evaluation Mode)




Dropout
Randomly disables neurons (adds noise)
All neurons active (no dropout)


BatchNorm
Uses current batch stats (mean/var)
Uses running (saved) stats


Gradient Tracking
Yes
Use with torch.no_grad() to disable


When to use
During training loop
During validation/testing/inference



Typical use:


Code
# train data
n_samples, m_features = 100, 10
x = torch.rand((n_samples, m_features))
y = torch.bernoulli(x[:,0]).reshape(-1,1) # y = 0 or 1


# Training
n_epochs = 10
model.train()
for epoch in range(n_epochs):

    output = model(x)           # forward path x -> output

    loss = loss_function(output, y) # loss calculation

    optimizer.zero_grad() # reset gradients (do not accumulate)
    loss.backward()       # backward path L -> x (gradient calc)
    optimizer.step()      # update parm = parm - learning_rate * grad

    # print loss
    if(epoch % 5 == 0):
        print('epoch {}, loss {}'.format(epoch, loss.data))

# Evaluation (Validation or Test or Prediction)
x_val = torch.rand((n_samples, m_features))
y_val = torch.bernoulli(x_val[:,0]).reshape(-1,1)

model.eval()
# disable grad calculation for speed
with torch.no_grad():
    val_output = model(x_val)
    val_loss = loss_function(val_output, y_val)


epoch 0, loss 0.257222056388855
epoch 5, loss 0.25282180309295654




Saving models
You may want to save (and share) your final model. But for long-running jobs it may also be good to save occasional checkpoints during training


Code
import os
save_dir = "checkpoints"
os.makedirs(save_dir, exist_ok=True)

# 1. only saves state_dict, need to know model class
torch.save(model.state_dict(), os.path.join(save_dir, "model_dict.pt"))
model.load_state_dict(torch.load("checkpoints/model_dict.pt"))

# 2. save full model. Warning: may not port across pytorch versions!!! 
torch.save(model, os.path.join(save_dir, "model_full.pth"))
model = torch.load("checkpoints/model_full.pth", weights_only=False)




Saving histories


Code
# 1. npz format (most general and robust)
np.savez(os.path.join(save_dir, "loss_history.npz"),
         train=train_losses, val=val_losses)

data = np.load("checkpoints/loss_history.npz")
train_losses = data["train"]
val_losses = data["val"]

# 2. pickle format (more flexible less portable)
with open(os.path.join(save_dir, "loss_history.pkl"), "wb") as f:
    pickle.dump({"train": train_losses, "val": val_losses}, f)

with open("checkpoints/loss_history.pkl", "rb") as f:
    losses = pickle.load(f)
train_losses = losses["train"]
val_losses = losses["val"]




Saving Everything


Code
torch.save({
    'model_state': model.state_dict(),
    'optimizer_state': optimizer.state_dict(),
    'train_losses': train_losses,
    'val_losses': val_losses,
}, os.path.join(save_dir, "checkpoint_all.pt"))

checkpoint = torch.load("checkpoints/checkpoint_all.pt")
model.load_state_dict(checkpoint['model_state'])
optimizer.load_state_dict(checkpoint['optimizer_state'])
train_losses = checkpoint['train_losses']
val_losses = checkpoint['val_losses']




Monitoring with Tensorboard (optional)
tensorboard is a popular tool to inspect neural networks and training progress


Code
from torch.utils.tensorboard import SummaryWriter

# default `log_dir` is "runs" - we'll be more specific here
writer = SummaryWriter('log_dir/mnist_experiment')

# typical uses
writer.add_graph(model)
for epoch in range(100):
    # training loop
    # ...
    # train_loss = loss_function(logits, y)
    writer.add_scalar("Loss", train_loss, epoch)

writer.close()

title: Perceptron
text:
Neuron Models



McCulloch and Pitts (1943)








A brief history of neuron++ models




McCulloch and Pitts (1943): Neurons as Boolean Gates (synaptic weights and activation thresholds)
Hebbs (1949): Neurons can learn weights; “fire together & wire together”
Rosenblatt (1957): A mathematical learning rule + a machine

title: Perceptron
text:
An Application: Binary Classification



Mark I Perceptron (1958). Distinguish pairs of letters (20x20 pixels) - with 80% accuracy!

title: Perceptron
text:
Perceptions and Citations
– Mechanisation of Thought Processes –
Rosenblatt (1957): “Devices of this sort are expected ultimately to be capable of concept formation, language translation, collation of military intelligence, and the solution of problems through inductive logic.”



New York Times, July 7 1958

title: Perceptron
text:
Boolean Gates & AI Winter
How to adjust weights and thresholds to calculate Boolean functions?



Boolean Gates with weights and thresholds. \(x_1, x_2, y \in \{0,1\}\).

title: Perceptron
text:
An Algorithm
Goal given a Boolean truth table \((x,y) \to\) find weights \(w_l\) and thresholds \(b_l\)



Perceptron Calculation. A linear collection function + a non-linear activation function.









Learning = updating weights



\[
w_{l} \to w_{l} + \alpha (y - \hat y)*x_l
\]

all Boolean \(x_l, y, \hat y \in \{0, 1\}\)
modified Hebbs learning: weights are updated only if target \(y\) is not yet reached: \(y \ne \hat y\)
online learning: one sample at a time
BUT: solution only for linearly separated data (not for XOR)

title: Perceptron
text:
Classification
For continuous \(x\), each neuron in a perceptron defines a (linear) decision boundary based on the value of \(z\).
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]



Decision Boundary for continous \(x\)

title: Perceptron
text:
Universality with Multiple Layers
With a multilayer perceptron (MLP) it is possible to calculate XOR, or any Boolean function



Multi-Layer-Perceptron to calculate XOR. Source: wikipedia








BUT




simple perceptron algorithm only works for single layer and linearly separaberable data.
need new algorithm: Backpropagation (Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams; Nature 1986)

title: Perceptron
text:
Complex Classification
Below I have hand-selected weights and thresholds for an MLP (with 3 hidden nodes) to create 3 decision boundaries that form a triangle.
Based on this I have sampled points \((x_1, x_2)\) and assigned 2 labels (0=outside triangle, 1=inside triangle)
Let’s explore if we can learn the known boundaries from just the data \((x,y)\).
We will discuss the algorithm later, but here I just want to illustrate that it seems to work.


Code
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# some convenience functions
from lecture_utils.helper import detect_device
from lecture_utils.perceptron_utils import  plot_data_and_boundaries, plot_decision_boundary, get_data, train_model

# define 3 decision boundaries with W and b
W = np.array([[2,-1],[-2,-1],[0,1]])
b = np.array([1,1,1]).reshape(1,-1)

# sample data and assign labels based on known boundaries
ns = 10000
X, labels = get_data(W, b, n_samples = ns)

# plot data and boundaries
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(10, 10))
title_str = f'True Boundaries and {ns} data points'
plot_data_and_boundaries(ax, X.cpu(), labels.cpu(), W, b, title=title_str)
plt.show()





boundary parameters (w1, w2, b) specify boundary equation: w1 x1 + w2 x2 + b = 0






Code
# a simple model with minimal number of layers (see data generation)
class MinimalModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(MinimalModel, self).__init__()

        self.hidden_layer = nn.Linear(2, 3)
        self.output_layer = nn.Linear(3, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.sigmoid(self.hidden_layer(x)) # sigmoid just for positivity
        x = torch.sigmoid(self.output_layer(x)) # Sigmoid activation for classification
        return x

# a complex model with 2 hidden layers
class ComplexModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(ComplexModel, self).__init__()

        self.hidden1 = nn.Linear(2, 16)
        self.hidden2 = nn.Linear(16,32)
        self.output_layer = nn.Linear(32, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.sigmoid(self.hidden1(x))
        x = torch.sigmoid(self.hidden2(x))
        x = torch.sigmoid(self.output_layer(x)) # Sigmoid activation for classification
        return x

bp = minimal_model.state_dict()['hidden_layer.bias'].cpu().numpy().reshape(1,-1)

Gradients \(\approx 0\) in early layers

Loss or parameters become NaN/∞

Small Data
Poor performance on small datasets

Want to make Gradient Descent faster and more robust

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Tip



Want to make Gradient Descent faster and more robust

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Use Mini-Batches
Calcuating the gradient for the full dataset can be expensive.

Break task and data into mini-batches: \(N_b < N\) (e.g. \(N_b = 64\))
gradient estimates will be more noisy

\[
\frac{dL}{dW} \approx g_t = \frac{1}{N_b}\sum_{i}^{N_b} \frac{dL_i}{dW}
\]
Notice: \(dL/dW\) denotes gradient vector for all parameters (weights and biases)






Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)




more rapid
more memory efficient
escape shallow local minima
noisy updates as regularization
early convergence
efficiency more important than exactness !

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Consider Momentum


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

w1 = np.linspace(-1, 1, 401)
w2 = np.linspace(-1, 1, 401)
W1, W2 = np.meshgrid(w1, w2)
sigma1 = 0.5   # wide in w1
sigma2 = 0.2   # narrow in w2
Z = 1 - np.exp(-((W1**2) / (2 * sigma1**2) + (W2**2) / (2 * sigma2**2)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
cont = plt.contourf(W1, W2, Z, cmap="Reds", levels=10)
ax.set_title("Loss Function (contour plot)")
ax.set_xlabel("w1")
ax.set_ylabel("w2")
plt.colorbar(cont, ax=ax, label="loss")
plt.show()





Not all parameters are created equal.





different parameters can have different impact on loss function
adjust learning rate or gradient depending on parameters (faster/slower learning)
prevent overshooting/oscillation in “narrow” directions







Exponential weighted averages



Average gradient estimates over minibatch iterations \(g_t \to v_t\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_t &= \beta_1 v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1) g_t \\
W &= W - \alpha v_t
\end{aligned}
\]

smoothed gradient (“velocity”)
new hyperparameter: e.g. \(\beta_1=0.9\)
weighted average over \(1/(1-\beta_1)\) batches
include friction term (\(\beta_1\)) and acceleration (\(1-\beta\))





Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
x = np.linspace(0,64,65)
eps = np.random.normal(0, 0.2, size=x.shape)
# simulate decay to true value (y=1)
W = 1 + np.exp(-0.5*(x+eps))
G = np.random.normal(1, 0.2, size=x.shape)

beta = 0.9
G_smooth = np.zeros_like(G)
G_smooth[0] = W[0]
for t in range(1, len(G)):
    G_smooth[t] = beta * G_smooth[t-1] + (1 - beta) * G[t]

plt.scatter(x,G, label="mini-batch")
plt.plot(x, G_smooth, color='orange',label="smoothed")
plt.title(f'Exponential Weighted Average (beta = {beta})')
plt.xlabel('minibatch t')
plt.ylabel('gradient')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()















Note




efficient implementation of averaging
bias for small time \(t\) (early minibatches)
averages out strong fluctuations

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
RMSProp (root-mean-square prop)
Similar idea; scale gradients by their squared running average
\[
\begin{aligned}
S_t &= \beta_2 S_{t-1} + (1-\beta_2) g_t^2 \\
W & = W - \alpha \left(\frac{g_t}{\sqrt{S_t}}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

\(g_t^2\): element-wise squares of gradient
update direction of \(W\) with small gradients stronger than large gradients
dampen oscillations in high gradient directions
smoother updates permit increased learning rate

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
ADAM (Adaptive Moment Estimation)
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_t &= \beta_1 v_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1) g_t \\
S_t &= \beta_2 S_{t-1} + (1-\beta_2) g_t^2 \\
W & = W - \alpha \left(\frac{v_t}{\sqrt{S_t}}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

combines Momentum with RMSprop
real ADAM also contains some bias correction
most commonly used in modern application: (\(\beta_1 = 0.9, \beta_2=0.999)\), tune \(\alpha\)

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Learning Rate Decay
Reduce learning steps closer to minimum
\[
\alpha_t =  \alpha_0 \left(\frac{1}{1+\tau*{\mbox epoch}}\right)
\]

Decay rate \(\tau\)
different ways to specify decay: exponential, manually

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Local Minima & Plateaus
(Local) Ninima: - curvature (second derivative) has to be positive into all directions - increasingly unlikely in high-dimensions
Saddle Point:

curvature positive into some direction, negative in others
increasingly likely in high-dimensions



Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

w1 = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
w2 = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
W1, W2 = np.meshgrid(w1, w2)

Z = W1**2 - W2**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))
cont = ax.contourf(W1, W2, Z, levels=30, cmap="RdBu_r") 
plt.colorbar(cont, ax=ax, label="f(w1, w2)")

ax.contour(W1, W2, Z, levels=[0], colors='black', linewidths=1.2)
ax.axhline(0, color='k', lw=0.8)
ax.axvline(0, color='k', lw=0.8)

ax.set_aspect('equal', 'box')
ax.set_xlabel(r"$w_1$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$w_2$")
ax.set_title("Saddle Point:  $Loss = w_1^2 - w_2^2$")
plt.show()















Note




In high dimensions of parameter space it is more common to have saddle points rather than local minima
Main concern is long plateaus = slow learning

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Parameter Initialization

different initialization can lead to different solutions
avoid node symmetry: random initialization, not zero
avoid large weights \(\to\) vanishing gradients (w/ sigmoid) \(\to\) no learning
avoid small weights \(\to\) small activation \(\to\) small gradients

\[
(\partial L / \partial w_i) = (\partial L / \partial a)   (\partial a / \partial w_i)
\]






Different Initializations at different layers:



For layer with \(n_l\) nodes \[
\begin{aligned}
z &= \sum_i w_i x_i \\
Var(z) &= n_l Var(w) Var(x)
\end{aligned}
\]
We want that Var(output) \(\propto\) Var(input)

Xavier Initialization (for tanh): \(Var(w) = 1/n_l\)
Kaiming Initialization (for ReLU); \(Var(w) = 2/n_l\)
many more: e.g. torch.nn.init

title: Engineering with Gradients
text:
Vectorization & GPU
Matrix Multiplication:

\(n\) samples
\(k\) input features
\(m\) output features

\[
\begin{aligned}
z &= X &\cdot& W &+ b\\
[n,m] &= [n,k] &\cdot& [k,m] &+ [m]
\end{aligned}
\]
Be aware of conventions and possible matrix transposition.
Using GPU:

def dataset2images(dataset, classes, SEED=42, n=16):
    """Show n random images from a dataset in a 4x4 grid."""
    np.random.seed(SEED)
    indices = np.random.choice(len(dataset), n, replace=False)

    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
    for i, idx in enumerate(indices):
        img, label = dataset[idx]

        if torch.is_tensor(img):
            # PyTorch tensor (C,H,W) -> numpy (H,W,C)
            img_np = img.permute(1, 2, 0).cpu().numpy()

        elif isinstance(img, Image.Image):
            # PIL Image
            img_np = np.array(img)

        elif isinstance(img, np.ndarray):
            # numpy array already OK
            img_np = img

        else:
            raise TypeError(
                f"Unsupported image type: {type(img)}. "
                "Expected torch.Tensor, PIL.Image, or numpy.ndarray."
            )

        ax = plt.subplot(4, 4, i + 1)
        ax.imshow(img_np)
        ax.set_title(f"{label}: {classes[label]}")
        ax.axis("off")

IMAGENET_MEAN = (0.485, 0.456, 0.406)
IMAGENET_STD  = (0.229, 0.224, 0.225)
IMAGENET_SIZE = 224

tfm = transforms.Compose([
    # transform to ImageNet Parameters to be used with ResNet
    transforms.Resize(256),
    transforms.CenterCrop(IMAGENET_SIZE),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(IMAGENET_MEAN, IMAGENET_STD),
])

tfm_aug = transforms.Compose([
    # transform and augment
    transforms.Resize(256),
    transforms.RandomRotation(degrees=20), # augmentation
    transforms.CenterCrop(IMAGENET_SIZE),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(IMAGENET_MEAN, IMAGENET_STD),
])



def denormalize(imgs, mean=IMAGENET_MEAN, std=IMAGENET_STD):
    """
    imgs: _normalized_ tensor of shape (B, C, H, W) or (C, H, W)
    mean, std: either scalars or sequences of length C
    Assumes imgs were normalized as (x - mean) / std.
    Purpose: visualize normalized imgs
    """
    # unsqueeze images if not already batch
    is_batched = (imgs.dim() == 4)
    if not is_batched:
        imgs = imgs.unsqueeze(0)   # (C,H,W) -> (1,C,H,W)

    B, C, H, W = imgs.shape

    # Turn mean/std into tensors on the correct device
    if isinstance(mean, (int, float)):
        mean = [mean] * C
    if isinstance(std, (int, float)):
        std = [std] * C

    mean = torch.tensor(mean, device=imgs.device, dtype=imgs.dtype).view(1, C, 1, 1)
    std  = torch.tensor(std,  device=imgs.device, dtype=imgs.dtype).view(1, C, 1, 1)

    imgs_denorm = imgs * std + mean
    imgs_denorm = imgs_denorm.clamp(0, 1)

    if not is_batched:
        imgs_denorm = imgs_denorm.squeeze(0)

    return imgs_denorm

Note

Code
from torchvision.models import resnet50, ResNet50_Weights
from torchinfo import summary

def build_resnet50_transfer(num_classes=1, freeze_backbone=True):
    # Load pretrained weights
    weights = ResNet50_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1
    model = resnet50(weights=weights)

    # freeze all parameters
    # do not calculate gradients, do not update
    if freeze_backbone:
        for param in model.parameters():
            param.requires_grad = False

    # Replace final fully connected layer
    # the new layer will have unfrozen (trainable) parameters
    in_features = model.fc.in_features
    model.fc = nn.Linear(in_features, num_classes)

    return model

# instantiate transfered model

Training

NUM_EPOCHS = 10 # keep small for faster execution
history = {'loss': [], 'val_loss': []}
# loop over epochs
for epoch in range(NUM_EPOCHS):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0
    # loop over batches
    for xb, yb in train_loader:
        # send batches to device
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        z_pred = model(xb)
        loss = loss_function(z_pred, yb)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()

    # record average loss over all batches
    train_loss = total_loss / len(train_loader.dataset)
    history['loss'].append(train_loss)

    # Validation
    model.eval()
    val_loss = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for xb, yb in val_loader:
            xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
            z_pred = model(xb)
            loss = loss_function(z_pred, yb)
            val_loss += loss.item()
    val_loss = val_loss / len(val_loader.dataset)
    history['val_loss'].append(val_loss)
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: train_loss={train_loss:.4f}, val_loss={val_loss:.4f}")

from lecture_utils.helper import plot_cm, detect_device

def plot_fit_history(hist, name='loss', test_score=None):
    if name not in hist:
        print(f"{name} not found in history")
        return
    plt.plot(hist[name], label='train')
    val_name = 'val_' + name
    if val_name in hist:
        plt.plot(hist[val_name], label='valid')
    if test_score is not None:
        plt.axhline(test_score, color='green', linestyle='-.', label='test')
    plt.xlabel('Epoch')
    plt.ylabel(name)
    plt.legend()
    plt.show()

def dataset2images(dataset, classes, SEED=42, n=16):
    """Show n random images from a dataset in a 4x4 grid."""
    np.random.seed(SEED)
    indices = np.random.choice(len(dataset), n, replace=False)

    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
    for i, idx in enumerate(indices):
        img, label = dataset[idx]

        if torch.is_tensor(img):
            # PyTorch tensor (C,H,W) -> numpy (H,W,C)
            img_np = img.permute(1, 2, 0).cpu().numpy()

        elif isinstance(img, Image.Image):
            # PIL Image
            img_np = np.array(img)

        elif isinstance(img, np.ndarray):
            # numpy array already OK
            img_np = img

        else:
            raise TypeError(
                f"Unsupported image type: {type(img)}. "
                "Expected torch.Tensor, PIL.Image, or numpy.ndarray."
            )

        # Plot
        ax = plt.subplot(4, 4, i + 1)
        ax.imshow(img_np)
        ax.set_title(f"{label}: {classes[label]}")
        ax.axis("off")

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

title: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Review

neural networks: layers of neurons connected by weights
each neuron: linear function (weights/bias) + non-linear activation function
loss optimization: iterations, (stochastic) gradient descent, momentum, ADAM
mathematical solution: backprogation of gradients = chain rule on computational graph
engineering challenges:

vanishing gradients \(\to\) activation functions, weight initialization
overfitting \(\to\) test set, regularization
coding complexity \(\to\) frameworks

interpretation challenges:

overconfidence and calibration
overparameterization, interpretation
beyond accuracy and other goals

title: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Another classical dataset: CIFAR-10
This is a set of 50k coloured images [32, 32, 3] in 10 categories.

Can also be obtain with torchvision.datasets.
Unlike MNIST, images are not as standardized.
Reference: Krizhevsky, Nair, Hinton



Code
from torchvision import datasets, transforms

transform = transforms.Compose([
    # can add many (image) transformations here
    # .... resize, rotate, flip, ....
    # minimal transformation: PIL --> tensor
    transforms.ToTensor(),
])

# tochvision dataset: list of tuples (tensor, int)
train_dataset = datasets.CIFAR10(root='./data', 
    train=True, download=True, transform=transform)

MB = train_dataset.data.nbytes / 1024**2

cifar_classes = train_dataset.classes
print(f"classes: {cifar_classes}")
print(f"train: shape={train_dataset.data.shape} size={MB:.2f} MB")

# plot a selection
dataset2images(train_dataset, cifar_classes)

Note




CIFAR-10 is downloaded to disk (./data/)
RAM footprint can be much larger for other image data (\(\to\) DataLoader)

Message




No apparent class structure (unlike MNIST)

change data representation and network architecture

title: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Discussion: Challenges ?



Confused Cat



spatially distributed features
occlusions / different view points
different sizes / resolutions
multiple classes
intraclass variation
deformation / orientation
illumination / colour choices

title: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Adding more layers ?

MLP does not scale to larger images
MLP do not encode spatial structure \(\to\) pixel permutations possible
MLP do not encode hierarchy (edges, shapes, textures, objects)
optimization increasingly hard







FC Problem in Numbers



1 input x 1 hidden layer:
[1000 x 1000 x 3 pixels] x [1000 neurons] \(\to\) 3 billion parameters

title: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
The idea

restrict capacity of each layer \(\to\) weight sharing
invariance assumption: it should not matter where feature is

We can also compose more complex transformations:

image size adjustments (if necessary)
number of channels match (RGB or gray scale?)
same normalization as done for model training



Code
# code not executed - just for illustration

# image sizes and channels
IMG_H, IMG_W, IMG_C = 32, 32, 3

# normalizations are part of model definition!
MEAN = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]
STD = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]

transform = transforms.Compose([
    # adjust H,W,C if necessary
    transforms.Resize((IMG_H, IMG_W)),
    transforms.Grayscale(num_output_channels=IMG_C),  

    transforms.ToTensor(),                    # image -> tensor
    transforms.Normalize(mean=MEAN, std=STD), # image norm
])

Transformations that do not change the label.

# define augmentation transformation
augment = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.RandomRotation(degrees=20),
    transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(p=0.5),  
    transforms.ColorJitter(brightness=0.3, contrast=0.3),
    transforms.RandomResizedCrop(size=512, scale=(0.8, 1.0)),
])

def imshow(img, title):
    # (C,H,W) -> (H,W,C) for visual with plt
    npimg = img.permute(1,2,0).numpy()
    plt.imshow(npimg)
    plt.title(title)
    plt.axis("off")

title: Data Augmentation

Augmentation with Datasets






Working with larger datasets




attach transformation function to the dataset
transformation will be called upon data access (not upon load)




Define transformation

# Modular definition: transformation + augmentation
transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.ToTensor(),  
    augment,       
])




Dataset from directory






Note




images are located in ROOT directory
organized by classes in subdirectories: cat/, dog/, …



For illustration: single image in single subfolder

title: Data Augmentation
text:
Augmentation with Data Loader
Usually we access datasets with DataLoaders.






Note




size of a dataset (and an epoch) becomes arbitrary
replace each original image with many generated images





Code
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, RandomSampler

NS=50_000     # number of samples to define epoch
BATCH_SIZE=64 # batch size for processing
print(f'orginal data size: {len(ds)}')

# sampling with replacement since we may have: len(ds) < NS
sampler = RandomSampler(ds, replacement=True, num_samples=NS)

train_loader = DataLoader(
    ds,
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE,
    sampler=sampler, # not shufffle=True
)

X,y = next(iter(train_loader))
print(f'batch sizes (with augmentation)') 
print(f'images: {X.shape}, labels: {y.shape}')

text:
Data Preparation
The data preparation invokes the usual steps:

splitting data sets: train, validation, test
optionally: downsample for speed
batch definition


Downsampling and Splitting
Downsampling is done for faster learning, but at the expense of accuracy


Code
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from torch.utils.data import Subset

FRACT = 0.1   # fraction to sample
TRAIN_VAL=0.8 # train-validation split

# get current sizes
train_indices = np.arange(len(train_dataset)) 
train_labels  = np.array(train_dataset.targets)
test_indices  = np.arange(len(test_dataset))  
test_labels   = np.array(test_dataset.targets)

# subsample fraction FRACT from train and test data 
train_pool_idx, _ = train_test_split(
    train_indices, train_size=FRACT, stratify=train_labels,
    random_state=SEED
)
test_idx, _ = train_test_split(
    test_indices, train_size=FRACT, stratify=test_labels, 
    random_state=SEED
)

# stratified split: train -> train + validation
y_pool = train_labels[train_pool_idx]
train_idx, val_idx = train_test_split(
    train_pool_idx, train_size=TRAIN_VAL, stratify=y_pool,
    random_state=SEED
)

# define subsets
train_ds = Subset(train_dataset, train_idx)
val_ds   = Subset(train_dataset, val_idx)
test_ds  = Subset(test_dataset,  test_idx)

print(f"train: {train_idx.shape}, val: {val_idx.shape}, test: {test_idx.shape}")


train: (4000,), val: (1000,), test: (1000,)




Batch Definition
DataLoaders provide efficient access to batches during training and evaluation


Code
# shuffle = True for randomized optimization and gradients calculation
train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True)

# shuffle = False for fixed and consistent evaluation and metrics
test_loader  = DataLoader(test_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=False)
val_loader   = DataLoader(val_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=False)

title: Implementation
text:
Model Definition (CNN)

Define Model Class
It will be useful to define the model class in a separate file that can be used reproducibly across contexts and notebooks.
Here I define a very simple CNN with a single convolutional layer.
Content of models/cnn.py:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class CNN_Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(3, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
        self.pool1 = nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2)
        self.flatten = nn.Flatten()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(32 * 16 * 16, 128)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x))
        x = self.pool1(x)
        x = self.flatten(x)
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x


Instantiate Model


Code
from torchinfo import summary
from models.cnn import CNN_Model

nc = len(cifar_classes)  # number of classes
model = CNN_Model(num_classes=nc).to(device)
loss_function = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters())

# inspect input shape, model, layers, params counts
X, _ = next(iter(train_loader))
print('input_shape:      ', X.shape)
print('number of classes:', nc)
print(summary(model, input_data=X, device=device))

title: Implementation
text:
Model Training
Training will take some time - especially without dedicated hardware (e.g. GPU).
In the following the number of epochs is kept low


Code
NUM_EPOCHS = 10 # keep small for faster execution
history = {'loss': [], 'val_loss': []}
# loop over epochs
for epoch in range(NUM_EPOCHS):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0
    # loop over batches
    for xb, yb in train_loader:
        # send batches to device
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        z_pred = model(xb)
        loss = loss_function(z_pred, yb)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()

    # record average loss over all batches
    train_loss = total_loss / len(train_loader.dataset)
    history['loss'].append(train_loss)

    # Validation
    model.eval()
    val_loss = 0
    with torch.no_grad():
        for xb, yb in val_loader:
            xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
            z_pred = model(xb)
            loss = loss_function(z_pred, yb)
            val_loss += loss.item()
    val_loss = val_loss / len(val_loader.dataset)
    history['val_loss'].append(val_loss)
    print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}: train_loss={train_loss:.4f}, val_loss={val_loss:.4f}")

doc_dir = os.getcwd()

# safe full model: unsafe pickle, discouraged

    "model_state": model.state_dict(),
    "optimizer_state": optimizer.state_dict(),
    "history": history,
    "epoch": epoch,
    "classes": cifar_classes,
    "seed": SEED,
    # append as necessary

}

title: Implementation
text:
Model Evaluations


Code
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay, classification_report

model.eval()
total_loss = 0
all_logits = []
all_true = []
with torch.no_grad():
    for xb, yb in test_loader:
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        z_pred = model(xb)
        loss = loss_function(z_pred, yb)

        # collect total_loss, predicted logits and true labels yb
        total_loss += loss.item()
        all_logits.append(z_pred.cpu())
        all_true.append(yb.cpu())

test_loss = total_loss / len(test_loader.dataset) # mean test loss
all_logits = torch.cat(all_logits)
all_true = torch.cat(all_true)

plot_fit_history(history, 'loss', test_loss)

all_preds = all_logits.argmax(dim=1).detach().cpu()
cm = confusion_matrix(all_true.numpy(), all_preds.numpy())

disp = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm)
disp.plot(cmap='Blues')
plt.show()

section: 8. Approximate inverse: estimate z from x
text:
8. Approximate inverse: estimate z from x


Code
from scipy.stats import norm, rankdata

# Empirical CDF of x
ranks = rankdata(x)
empirical_cdf = ranks / (len(x) + 1)
z_hat = norm.ppf(empirical_cdf)

# Compare distribution
plt.hist(z_hat, bins=40, density=True, alpha=0.6, label="z_hat ~ N(0,1)")
x_vals = np.linspace(-3, 3, 200)
plt.plot(x_vals, norm.pdf(x_vals), 'k--', label="True N(0,1)")
plt.legend()
plt.title("Transformed variable z_hat from x")
plt.show()




Code
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

model = make_pipeline(PolynomialFeatures(degree=10), LinearRegression())
model.fit(z_hat.reshape(-1, 1), x)

z_test = np.linspace(-3, 3, 300)
x_pred = model.predict(z_test.reshape(-1, 1))

plt.plot(z_test, x_pred, label="Learned g(z)")
plt.scatter(z, x, alpha=0.2, s=10, label="True (z, x)")
plt.xlabel("z")
plt.ylabel("x")
plt.title("Learned inverse mapping z → x")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

This example shows how a nonlinear, complex distribution p(x) can be constructed from a simple latent variable z ~ N(0,1). text:
3. Indexing and slicing
Python uses zero-based indexing.

X[0, 0] means first row, first column
X[1, :] means second row, all columns
X[:, 2] means all rows, third column



Code
print('Top-left element:', X[0, 0])
print('Second row:', X[1, :])
print('Third column:', X[:, 2])

Second row: [4 5 6]
Third column: [3 6 9]

title: Tutorial Template: NumPy Basics and a First Look at PyTorch

4. Element-wise operations
Many operations in NumPy are applied element by element.


Code

print('A      =', A)

print('A + y  =', A + y)
print('A * 2  =', A * 2)
print('A ** 2 =', A ** 2)

Sometimes is useful to access datasets as dataframes (for data explorations and plotting). This can be done by resetting the format:


Code
# changes __getitem__ access method: 
# arrowtable --> dataframe
ds.set_format("pandas") # ds[0] return dataframes

# typical pandas application

HF dataset use Apache Arrow format for very fast access
1. standardize data access
2. zero copy and parallel access 
3. every dataset is a memory map file


Code
from datasets import load_dataset

# loads ds & stores in arrow format (locally)
ds = load_dataset("cifar10")
ds.cache_files  # show local cache

# save to directory (arrow)
ds.save_to_disk(output_dir)  
# fast load from directory (arrow)
ds.load_from_disk(input_dir)


Save specific subsets (train, test, …)


Code
# save as csv for smaller individual data set
ds["train"].to_csv("train.csv", index=None)  
# save as parquet for long-term efficient storage 
ds["train"].to_parquet("train.parquet")      

# or loop over indivdual subsets
for split, ds_split in ds.items():
    ds_split.to_csv(f"split-{split}.csv", index=None)








Aspect
Arrow
Parquet




Primary role
In-memory format
On-disk storage format


Memory mapping
Yes (core feature)
No (must decode)


Random row access
Very fast
Slower


Parsing cost
None (zero-copy)
Required (decode + decompress)


File size
Larger (uncompressed)
Much smaller (compressed)


Typical use
Fast access, ML pipelines
Data lakes, analytics, archives


Link
Arrow
Parquet






for huge data that does not fit into memory (or hard drive)


Code
# streamed dataset _cannot_ be indexed directly
ds_iterable = load_dataset(ds_name, streaming=True) 
next(iter(ds_iterable))           # use iterator
ds_train = ds_iterable.skip(1000) # skip first 1000 (for train)
ds_test = ds_iterable.take(1000)  # take first 1000 (for test)





Ultimately we want to use datasets with (image) transformations and DataLoaders for batch processing.
In PyTorch, we can extend the Dataset class to return transformed image tensors when requested with __getitem__


Code
import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset

class HFCIFAR10(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, hf_ds, transform=None):
        self.hf_ds = hf_ds       # e.g. ds["train"]
        self.transform = transform

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.hf_ds)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        example = self.hf_ds[idx]
        img = example["img"]           # PIL
        label = int(example["label"])  # label -> int

        if self.transform is not None:
            # transform PIL Image to torch.Tensor (3,H,W)
            # + other transforms specified by transform() 
            img = self.transform(img)

        # return (transformed) image and label
        return img, label


As before, it is important to define systematic data transformation that are applied consitently to all batches


Code
transform = transforms.Compose([
    # add and adjust as needed ...
    transforms.ToTensor(),
])

train_ds = HFCIFAR10(train_hf, transform=transform)
test_ds = HFCIFAR10(test_hf, transform=transform)
print(type(train_ds))
img, label = train_ds[0]
print(f'img: {type(img)} {img.shape} label: {label}')





… can be defined as before


Code
BATCH_SIZE = 64
train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True)
test_loader  = DataLoader(test_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=False)

X_train, y_train = next(iter(train_loader))
print(f'X_train: {X_train.shape}, y_train= {y_train.shape}')

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.hf_ds)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        example = self.hf_ds[idx]
        img = example["img"]           # PIL
        label = int(example["label"])  # label -> int

        if self.transform is not None:
            # transform PIL Image to torch.Tensor (3,H,W)
            # + other transforms specified by transform() 
            img = self.transform(img)

        # return (transformed) image and label
        return img, label


As before, it is important to define systematic data transformation that are applied consitently to all batches

title: Convolutions
text:
Convolutions combine two functions by sliding one (the kernel \(g(x)\)) over the other
An image:



1D Convolution


A formula: \[
(f \ast g)(x) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} f(k) \cdot g(x-k)
\]
sum is over all \(k\), but in practice \(g(x-k) = 0\) when \(|x-k| \gg 0\)
This can be used conveniently for

smoothing/averaging
change detection (derivatives)
pattern detection
sharpening



Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.signal import convolve

# Create a signal with edges
x = np.linspace(-10, 10, 400)
f = np.sin(x) + 0.1*np.random.randn(len(x))  # noisy sinus

# Kernels
xk = np.linspace(-3, 3, 51)
sigma = 1
g1 = np.exp(-(xk**2)/(2*sigma**2)); 
g1 = g1 / g1.sum()             # Gaussian for smoothing/averaging
g2 = np.array([-1, 1])         # 1st derivative
g3 = np.array([1, -2, 1])      # 2nd derivative (Laplacian)
g4 = np.array([-1, 2, -1])     # High-pass filter (sharpening)

# Convolve
fs = convolve(f, g1, mode='same') # smoothed signal
f1 = convolve(fs, g2, mode='same') # 1st derivative
f1s = convolve(f1, g1, mode='same') # smoothed 1st derivativ
f2 = convolve(f1s, g3, mode='same') # 2nd derivative

# remove boundary effects
pad = 81
x = x[pad:-pad]
f = f[pad:-pad]
fs = fs[pad:-pad]
f1 = f1[pad:-pad]
f2 = f2[pad:-pad]

# Plot: 
# kernels are scaled arbitrarly for visibility
fsize = 12
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))
plt.subplot(4,1,1)
plt.plot(x, f)
plt.title("Original Data", fontsize=fsize)

plt.subplot(4,1,2)
plt.plot(x, fs)
plt.bar(xk-1, g1*20, color="orange", width=0.05)
plt.title("Smoothing/Averaging: fs = f * G  \n Gaussian Kernel $G \\propto \\exp{(-x^2)}$", fontsize=fsize)

plt.subplot(4,1,3)
plt.plot(x, f1); 
plt.bar(x[[99,100]], g2/20, color="orange",width=0.05)
plt.title("Change Detection: f1 = fs * D \n 1st derivative D = [-1, +1]", fontsize=fsize)

plt.subplot(4,1,4)
plt.plot(x, f2); 
plt.bar(x[[99,100,101]], g3/10000, color="orange",width=0.05)
plt.title("Curvature Detection: f2 = (f1 * G) * L \n 2nd derivative L = [1, -2, 1]", fontsize=fsize)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





1D data (blue) and kernels (orange).










Note




appropriate convolutions can extract features
many different kernels possible
find different (more suitable) data representations
kernel rules are shift-invariant and independent of data size
controlled by few parameters

title: Convolutions
text:
2D Conv

Illustration



2D Convolution. Source/Credits: https://anhreynolds.com/blogs/cnn.html




Real Examples
Below I use 2 filters for vertical and horizontal edge detection. \[
K_1 =
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 0 & 1 \\
-1 & 0 & 1 \\
-1 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\qquad
K_2 =
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & -1 & -1 \\
0  & 0  & 0  \\
1  & 1  & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\]


Code
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
from scipy.signal import convolve2d

transform = transforms.ToTensor()
dataset = datasets.CIFAR10(root="./data", train=False, download=True, transform=transform)

img, label = dataset[24]              # RGB img: [3,32,32]
img_gray = img.mean(dim=0).numpy()   # grey img: [32, 32]

K1 = np.array([
    [-1,  0,  1],
    [-1,  0,  1],
    [-1,  0,  1]
], dtype=float)
K2 = np.array([
    [-1,  -1,  -1],
    [0,  0,  0],
    [1,  1,  1]
], dtype=float)

img1 = convolve2d(img_gray, K1, mode="same", boundary="symm")
img2 = convolve2d(img_gray, K2, mode="same", boundary="symm")

img1 = (img1 - img1.min()) / (img1.max() - img1.min())
img2 = (img2 - img2.min()) / (img2.max() - img2.min())

plt.figure(figsize=(9,3))

plt.subplot(1, 3, 1)
plt.imshow(img_gray, cmap='gray')
plt.title("original CIFAR-10 image (gray)")
plt.axis("off")

plt.subplot(1, 3, 2)
plt.imshow(img1, cmap='gray')
plt.title("vertical edge filter K1")
plt.axis("off")

plt.subplot(1, 3, 3)
plt.imshow(img2, cmap='gray')
plt.title("horizontal edge filter K2")
plt.axis("off")

plt.show()















Note




Each filter creates a new “feature map”
CNN will learn filter weights
many different filters and corresponding feature maps
many layers of filters
emergence of “objects” and “concepts” in deep layers





Generalization & New Jargon



Convolution of RGB input with 2 filters. Filter depth matches input channels.



input channels: RGB images [C=3, H, W]
kernels=filters=neurons: [K,C,3,3]

\(3 \times 3\) filter: C input channels \(\to\) K output channels (depth)
K different filters for different patterns (e.g. horizontal, vertical lines)
filters have same channel depth as input
filter parameters are not manually defined, but they will be trained
filters/neurons scans all image patches with same parameters
Convolutions followed by non-linear activation

\[
Z = W \ast X + b \to ReLU(Z)
\]



More Jargon: Padding & Pooling



Convolutions: Padding & Pooling



padding

avoid size reduction
padding types: zero/constant, replicate, reflect, …

pooling filter (Pool)

spatial downsampling
channel depth stays the same
pooling type: max, average




Outputs Dimensions from Conv Layer
Output Size: \[
L_{out} = \frac{L_{in} - F + 2P}{S} + 1
\]






Note




\(L_{in}, L_{out}\) spatial dimensions of input and output images: \((H,W)\)
\(F=\) filter size, \(S=\) stride, \(P=\) padding
\((L_{in} - F + 2P)/S\) has to be integer for valid convolutional layer





Receptive Field



Receptive Field of \(3 \times 3\) filters. Source: N. Adaloglou (AI Summer 2020)



denotes the effective number of input pixels that contribute to activation
stacks of convolutional layers increase receptive field
early convolutional layers have small receptive fields (e.g \(3 \times 3\)): \(\to\) edge detection
deeper convolutional layers “see” more global structure \(\to\) object detection + classification accuracy

title: Convolutions
text:
Habits & Recommendations

use input size \(L \times L = 2^n\) (e.g. 512)
use stride \(S=1\)
use padding \(P= (F-1)/2\) to retain input size \(\to\) multiple CONV layers
Typical structures: Input - Conv/Relu - Conv/Relu - Pool - Conv/Relu - … - Dense

title: Convolutions
text:
A special one: 1x1 Convolutions
\(K \times K\) convolutions reduce spatial dimensions and increase channel depth (capactiy)
For very deep networks we may also want to reduce (summarize) channels (\(C \to K\)) without affecting spatial structures.



1x1 Convolution. Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wf2HblQbP-U


\[
y_{ij}^{(k)} = \sum_{c=1}^C w_c^{(k)} x_{ijc} + b_k
\]






Note




efficient way to summarize feature
“feature map pooling”: pool only across channels
learnable weight \(w_c^{(k)}\) (in contrast to spatial pooling: Max/Average)

Dealing with ReLU
Notice that the ReLU of the VGG16 model are shown as ReLU(inplace=True) In other words; ReLU(x) overwrites the orginal tensor \(x\).
This saves memory, but later we will need access to the original activations (and gradients). Therefore we set inplace=False.

        m.inplace = False

title: CNN Interpretation
text:
Optimizing Input: Activation Maximization
For each layer \(l\) and channel \(c\) we can define the mean activation (single scalar) for a given input image \(x\) \[
J_{l,c}(x) = \frac{1}{HW} \sum_{ij} f_{l,c}(x)
\]






Idea



optimize input image \(x\) to maximize \(J_{l,c}(x)\)


\[
\underset{x}{\mbox{argmax}} \left( J_{l,c}(x) - \lambda R(x) \right)
\]






Notes




c.f.loss minimization \(L(w)\): optimize \(w\)!
network parameters \(w\) fixed
gradient ascent: iterative optimization starting from random image
dependence on definition of \(J_{l,c}\) (other definitions possible)
dependence on regularization \(R(x)\) (smoothness, priors etc)
independent of data set !









Implementations




Lucid (TF 2018) distill.pub (Olah et al.)
Lucent (PyTorch 2020) (L. Kiat & T. Lieberum)






Activation Maximization for 4 channels of first and last convolutional layers (feature maps: 0 and 28)








Uses




detect dead filters
compare models

title: CNN Interpretation
text:
Interprete Predictions
Given a class prediction \(x \to c\), how would changes in one layer effect the output value.

Saliency


Code
# track gradients with respect to x
x = x.clone().detach().requires_grad_(True)

model.eval()       # set model into evaluation mode
model.zero_grad()  # zero all gradients

logits = model(x)                          # logits [1,1000]
c_id = logits.argmax(dim=1).item()         # pred. class
print('class: ', c_id, class_labels[c_id])

score = logits[0,c_id]     # class prediction score
score.backward()           # backward prop to calculate

saliency = x.grad.detach().abs()                  # [1,3,H,W]
saliency = saliency.max(dim=1, keepdim=True)[0]   # [1,1,H,W] max over c
saliency = saliency.detach()[0,0].cpu().numpy()   # [H,W]
# Normalize to [0,1]
saliency = (saliency - saliency.min()) / (saliency.max() - saliency.min() + 1e-8)

# Keep original image for overlay [H,W,C]
# resize to match x and normalize to [0,1]
img_for_overlay = img.resize(x.shape[2:])
img_for_overlay = np.float32(img_for_overlay) / 255.0

plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
plt.subplot(1,2,1); plt.imshow(img_for_overlay) 
plt.axis('off'); plt.title('Original')
plt.subplot(1,2,2); plt.imshow(saliency, cmap='gray')
plt.axis('off'); plt.title('Saliency')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()


class:  386 African elephant











Grad-CAM






Principle Idea



Importance of a pixel \((i,j)\) is related to

size of activation scores in last layer \(A_{ij}^k\)
importance of changes in score for class prediction \(c\): gradient \(\delta y^c / \delta A_{ij}^k\)
weighted average over all channel contributions from last conv layer



\[
L_{ij} = ReLU\left(\sum_k w_k A_{ij}^k\right)
\]
Some heuristics:

consider only positive contributions (\(ReLU\))
channel weight \(w_k\) (spatial average of gradients)

\[
w_k = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i,j} \frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A_{ij}^k} = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{i,j} g_{ij}^k~~~(Z=H'W')
\]
(usually we focus on a single class: drop \(c\) for simplcity)
Activations \(A_{ij}^k\) and gradients \(g_{ij}^k\) from forward and backpropagation.



Graphical Summary. GradCam vs. Saliency.








More heuristics: Grad-Cam++





Estimates for \(w_k\) can be improved
\[
w_k = \sum_{i,j} \alpha_{ij}^k ReLU(g_{ij}^k)
\]
with pixel specific weights
\[
\alpha_{ij}^k = \frac{(g_{ij}^k)^2}{ 2(g_{ij}^k)^2 + A^k (g_{ij}^k)^3 + \epsilon} ~~~~~A^k = \sum_{a,b} A_{ab}^k
\]
The goals with this are:

pixels with large positive gradient should contribute more to the weight.
globally active channels should be downgraded because we want localization: discourage large \(A^k\) in denominator
keep only positive evidence: \(ReLU(g_{ij})\)






Grad-Cam (with pytorch-grad-cam)
References:

package repo: https://github.com/jacobgil/pytorch-grad-cam
eigenCAM: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.00299



Code
from pytorch_grad_cam import (
    GradCAM, HiResCAM, ScoreCAM, GradCAMPlusPlus, 
    AblationCAM, XGradCAM, EigenCAM, FullGrad
)
from pytorch_grad_cam.utils.model_targets import ClassifierOutputTarget
from pytorch_grad_cam.utils.image import show_cam_on_image

target_layers = [ model.features[28] ]     # last conv for VGG16
with GradCAMPlusPlus(model=model, target_layers=target_layers) as cam:

  grayscale_cam = cam(input_tensor=x, targets=[ClassifierOutputTarget(c_id)])
  grayscale_cam = grayscale_cam[0, :]
  cam_on_image = show_cam_on_image(img_for_overlay, grayscale_cam, use_rgb=True)
  model_outputs = cam.outputs

  plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
  plt.subplot(1,2,1); plt.imshow(img_for_overlay)
  plt.axis('off'); plt.title('Original')
  plt.subplot(1,2,2); plt.imshow(cam_on_image) 
  plt.axis('off'); plt.title('Grad-CAM++')
  plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()















Warning



Some of these results look very promissing, but such “successes” are not very robust and depend on heuristical trial and error.
Try to change:

GradCamPlusPlus \(\to\) GradCam
cam(…, aug_smooth=True)
EigenCAM, …

text:
CNN Pathology

Filters (Kernels)


Code
# Get the trained weights of the 1st conv layer 
# shape: [out_channels, in_channels, H, W]
W = model.conv1.weight.data.cpu().numpy()   
print('W.shape: ',W.shape)   # [32, 3, 3, 3]

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
nr, nc = (4, 4)  # number of rows and columns
for i in range(nr * nc):
    # Each filter: shape [3, 3, 3] (in_channels, H, W)
    img = W[i].transpose(1, 2, 0) # transpose to (H,W,C)
    # Normalize to [0, 1] for visualization
    img = (img - img.min()) / (img.max() - img.min() + 1e-5)
    ax = fig.add_subplot(nr, nc, i + 1)
    ax.imshow(img)
    ax.set_title(f'filter: {i}')
    ax.axis('off')

plt.show()


W.shape:  (32, 3, 3, 3)















Note




shown is only a subset of all filters in the first conv layer
each 3x3 filter has 3 channels (to match the RGB channels from input) \(\to\) color representation
black/white: max/min activation for all filter channels
in general: filter channels match channels of input activation (different for different layers)
filter = neuron calculate \(z=W \ast x + b\) for each 3x3 image patch (+ padding)
filter parameters are not very helpful to understand predictive power





Feature Maps & Hooks
Goal of convolutional layers: obtain better data representation of the image data by representing spatial features (edges etc.).
With PyTorch we can use hooks to access the output from selected layers.


Code
def get_feature_map(model, dataloader, layer_name, device):
    """
    obtain representation of input data at the model layer defined by `layer_name`
    Use a `hook` to return the layer activations from forward calculation
    """
    model.eval()
    X_repr = []

    # define hook function that collects output from 
    # the layer (=module) at which it was registered
    # output will be appended to X_repr
    def hook(module, inp, out):
        X_repr.append(out.detach().cpu())

    # define layer of interest (by layer_name)
    layer = dict(model.named_modules())[layer_name]
    # register hook function at specified layer
    handle = layer.register_forward_hook(hook)

    # get data (xb, yb) from dataloader
    xb, yb = next(iter(dataloader))
    xb = xb.to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        _ = model(xb)

    handle.remove() # remove hook
    return xb.cpu().numpy(), X_repr[0].numpy(), yb.numpy()

# example use
X_orig, X_layer, y = get_feature_map(
    model,
    dataloader=train_loader,
    layer_name="conv1", 
    device=device,
)
print(f'X_orig.shape {X_orig.shape} X_layer.shape {X_layer.shape}')


X_orig.shape (500, 3, 32, 32) X_layer.shape (500, 32, 32, 32)




plotting
selected_channels = np.arange(12)
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))

# originals
plt.subplot(4, 4, 1)
plt.imshow(X_orig[i].transpose(1,2,0))
plt.title(f"Original")
plt.axis("off")
for j in range(3):
    plt.subplot(4, 4, 2+j)
    img = X_orig[i, j]
    plt.imshow(img, cmap="gray")
    plt.title(f"Orig, channel {j}")
    plt.axis("off")

# feature map
for k, ch in enumerate(selected_channels):
    plt.subplot(4, 4, 5+k)
    feature = X_layer[i, ch]
    plt.imshow(feature, cmap="gray")
    plt.title(f"Feature Map {ch}")
    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()















Summary




goal: ‘better’ representation of input data
MLP: pass 1D vectors from layer to layer
CNN: match network to spatial structure (pass 2D images)
parameter sharing: filter weights are independent of spatial location
Lower layers: Primitive “concepts” (edges)
Deeper layers: Higher order “concepts” (objects)

title: Non-linearities
text:
… for multinomial classification with \(K\) classes (in output layer)
\[
z_k = w_k \cdot x + b_k \longrightarrow p_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{l=0}^{K-1} e^{z_l}}~~~~k \in [0, ..., K-1]
\]

shift invariance (for probabilities):
\(p_k = f(z_k) = f(z_k + c)\)
scale invariance (for class predictions): \(\hat y_k = \arg \max_k f(z_k) = \arg \max_k f(z_k/T) =\arg \max_k z_k\)



Code
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import torch.nn.functional as F
from itertools import product

z = torch.tensor([-0.35, 1.5])
C = [0.0, 33.3, 0.0, 0.0]
T = [1.0, 1.0, 5.0, 0.50]
labels = [f"c={c}, T={t}" for c, t in zip(C,T)]
probs = torch.stack([F.softmax((z + c)/t, dim=0) for c, t in zip(C,T)])
x = torch.arange(len(labels)).numpy()
p0 = probs[:, 0].numpy()
p1 = probs[:, 1].numpy()

# --- stacked barplot ---
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.bar(x, p0, label="class 0", color="steelblue")
plt.bar(x, p1, bottom=p0, label="class 1", color="orange")
plt.axhline(0.5, color="gray", linestyle="--", linewidth=1)

plt.xticks(x, labels, rotation=30, ha="right")
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.ylabel("Probability")
plt.title("Softmax probabilities (with Shifts and Scales)")
plt.legend(loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Shift invariance of probabilities, Scale invariance of predictions, Confidence and Over-Confidence. Here for 2 categories with z = [-0.35, +1.5].










Note




interprete output of NN as probabilities with \(\sum p_k = 1\)
softmax uses all neurons (of output layer)
no hyper-parameter





How to avoid overflow \(e^{z_k}\) for large \(|z_k|\) ?
Define maximal \(z_k\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
z^\ast &= \max_k z_k \\
\log\sum_l e^{z_l} &= z^\ast + \log \sum_l e^{z_l - z^\ast}
\end{aligned}
\]
This trick is used by F.softmax(z) and other professional implementations of softmax.

title: Non-linearities
text:
… for multinomial classification with \(K\) classes (in output layer)
\[
z_k = w_k \cdot x + b_k \longrightarrow p_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{l=0}^{K-1} e^{z_l}}~~~~k \in [0, ..., K-1]
\]

shift invariance (for probabilities):
\(p_k = f(z_k) = f(z_k + c)\)
scale invariance (for class predictions): \(\hat y_k = \arg \max_k f(z_k) = \arg \max_k f(z_k/T) =\arg \max_k z_k\)

title: Non-linearities
text:
Sigmoid
… a special case for two output variables: binary logistic regression
\[
\left[z_0, z_1\right] \to \left[ p_0 = \frac{e^{z_0}}{e^{z_0} + e^{z_1}}, p_1 = 1 - p_0 \right] \\
\]
Binary Classification can be realized with single output neuron
\[
p_0 = \sigma(z_0) = \frac{1}{1+\exp(-z_0)}
\]






Binary Cross Entropy




special case of Cross Entropy 2 classes (binary output).

Loss for a single sample \((x,y) \to (z, y) \to (p, y) \to (\hat y, y)\)
\[
L(p, y) = - \sum_l y_l \log p_l = - y_0 \log p_0 - (1-y_0) \log( 1 - p_0)
\]
True class label: \(y_0 \in \{0, 1\}\) and \(y_1 = 1 - y_0\)
Total loss = average loss for all samples \((x_i,y_i)\)
\[
L = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i L(p_i, y_i)
\]

input: \(-\infty \ldots +\infty\)
output: \(0 \ldots 1\) (probability interpretation)
sharp transitions, position determined by bias
narrow range of changes & asymptotically flat
sigmoid can also be used for internal neurons
sigmoid works independently for individual neurons
no additional hyper-parameter

title: Non-linearities
text:
Other Activations Functions
Introduce non-linearities also for internal layers
2-layer network without non-linearities
\[
\begin{aligned}
z_1 & = W_1 \cdot x + b_1 \\
z_2 & = W_2 \cdot z_1 + b_2 \\
& = W_2 ( W_1 \cdot x + b_1) + b_2 \\
&= W^\prime x + b^\prime
\end{aligned}
\]
… useless and corresponds to 1-layer network !






Use (non-linear) activation functions \(a(z)\)



\[
\begin{aligned}
z_1 & = W_1 \cdot x + b_1 \\
a_1 & = a(z_1) \\
z_2 & = W_2 \cdot a_1 + b_2 \\
a_2 & = a(z_2)
\end{aligned}
\]

many activation functions \(a(z)\) are possible
should be simple and differentiable




tanh
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tanh(z) &= \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}} = 2 \sigma(2z) - 1
\end{aligned}
\]

title: Loss and Gradients
text:
Find loss minimum numerically and efficiently.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))

# define truth (possibly simple)
def y_true_fn(theta, x):
    y_true = ( theta[0] + 
        theta[1]*np.sin(theta[2] * x + theta[3]) +
        theta[4]*np.cos(theta[4] * x - theta[6]) +
        theta[7]*np.sin(theta[8] * x + theta[9])
    )
    # complexity of true model is irrelevant for shape of loss surface
    y_true = theta[0] + theta[1]*x 

#   optional: turn into binary
#    y_true = (y_true > np.median(y_true)).astype(int) 
    return y_true

# define model predictions (complex)
def y_pred_fn(w1, w2, x):
#    y_pred = sigmoid(w1 + w2 * x)
    y_pred = np.sin(np.pi*x + np.sin(w1*np.pi*x)) * np.cos(w2*x)
    return y_pred

def MSEloss(w1, w2):
    y_pred = y_pred_fn(w1, w2, x[:, None, None])
    return np.mean((y_true[:, None, None] - y_pred)**2, axis=0)

def CEloss(w1, w2):
    y_pred = y_pred_fn(w1, w2, x[:, None, None])
    eps = 1e-12 # avoid log(0)
    y_pred = np.clip(y_pred, eps, 1 - eps)
    # binary cross entropy
    bce = -(y_true[:, None, None] * np.log(y_pred) +
            (1 - y_true[:, None, None]) * np.log(1 - y_pred))
    return np.mean(bce, axis=0)

n = 240
x = np.linspace(-np.pi, np.pi, n)
theta = [9, 1.2, 3.0, 0.3, -0.8, 5.0, -0.1, 0.6, 7.0, 0.7, 0.2]
y_true = y_true_fn(theta,x)

#plt.figure(figsize=(6, 5))
#plt.scatter(x,y_true)
#plt.show()

# construct 2D parameter grid
w1 = np.linspace(-6, 6, 200)
w2 = np.linspace(-6, 6, 200)
W1, W2 = np.meshgrid(w1, w2)

Z = MSEloss(W1, W2)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,5))
plt.contourf(W1, W2, Z, levels=100, cmap='viridis')
plt.colorbar(label='Loss')
plt.xlabel('w1')
plt.ylabel('w2')
plt.title('Non-convex Loss Surface')
plt.show()















Note




Challenge: find (global) minimum for non-convex landscapes
Non-convexity is problem of (non-linear) model, not the complex data distribution
need efficient numerical algorithm to navigate in complex loss landscapes

title: Loss and Gradients
text:
Gradients






Idea & Hope



use local properties of loss lanscape to update parameters




Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Define the loss function and its derivative (gradient)
def L(w):
    return (w - 2)**2 + 1   # Parabola with minimum at w=2

def dL(w):
    return 2 * (w - 2)

# Choose a point away from the minimum
w0 = 0.5
L0 = L(w0)
grad = dL(w0)

# Define tangent line at w0
def tangent(w):
    return L0 + grad * (w - w0)

# Create range for plotting
w = np.linspace(-1, 3, 200)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
plt.plot(w, L(w), label='Loss $L(w)$', color='blue')
plt.plot(w, tangent(w), '--', color='red', label='Tangent at $w_0$')
plt.scatter([w0], [L0], color='red', zorder=5, label=f'initial $w_0 = {w0}$')
plt.scatter([2], [L(2)], color='green', zorder=5, label='Minimum')

# Annotate gradient direction
plt.arrow(w0, L0, 0.6, grad*0.6, head_width=0.1, head_length=0.3, fc='red', ec='red')

# Labels and style
plt.xlabel('Weight $w$')
plt.ylabel('Loss $L(w)$')
plt.title('Simple Loss Function')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





A more realistic loss function will depend on many weights and have non-convex shape










Gradient Descent




changes in parameter \(w\) will propagates through network to affect \(L\)
Parameter Update \[
w = w - \alpha \frac{dL}{dw}
\]
similar for bias term \(b\)
local derivative: \(\frac{dL}{dw} = f(w)\)
vectorization: \(w\) and \(\frac{dL}{dw}\) are vectors of same dimension. Derivatives \(\to\) Gradients
\(\alpha\): learning rate (hyperparameter)




Important Derivatives
How to calculate local slopes







Function \(f(x)\)
Derivative \(f'(x)=\frac{df}{dx}\)




\(5x\)
\(5\)


\(5x^2\)
\(10x\)


\(e^x\)
\(e^x\)


\(e^{-x}\)
\(-e^{-x}\)


Sigmoid: \(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}\)
\(\frac{e^{-x}}{(1 + e^{-x})^2} = f(x)(1 - f(x))\)


\(\tanh(x)\)
\(1 - f(x)^2\)


ReLU: \(max(0, x)\)
\((0,1)\)


\(f(g(x))\)
\(f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) \longrightarrow\) chain rule

title: Loss and Gradients
text:
Gradient Descent
each sample \(i\) contributes to overall loss and overall gradient \[
\begin{aligned}
L(w) &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_i L_i(w) \\
\frac{dL}{dw} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \frac{dL_i}{dw} \\
w &= w - \alpha \frac{d L}{dw}
\end{aligned}
\]

Initialization (random): \(w_0\)
step-wise update is expensive (sum over all data)
in practice this is done for **batches* of training samples

title: Loss and Gradients
text:
Forward Calculation

Example: Logistic regression



Logistic regression with two input \((x_1, x_2\)) and one output activation \(a \in [0,1]\). This network encodes a computation that is applied to each input sample \((x_1, x_2)\).


The loss function (binary cross entropy): \[
L(a,y) = -y \log(a) - (1-y)\log(1-a)
\]






Minimal Loss = Maximal Likelihood






observed data: \(x\)
true label: \(y\)
predicted label probability: \(p =f(x, \theta)\)

Conditional Probability: \[
Pr(\mbox{Label}=y|x, \theta) = p^y (1-p)^{(1-y)} \to \mbox{maximize !}
\]
Loss for one sample \(x\): \[
L(p, y) = - \log Pr(y|x, \theta) =  - y \log p - (1-y) \log (1 - p) \to \mbox{minimize !}
\]
Loss for all \(N\) samples \((x_1,y_1), (x_2,y_2) \ldots (x_N, y_N)\) and predicted probabilities \(p_i = f(x_i, \theta)\): \[
L(x,y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i L(p_i, y_i)
\]





Computational Graph



Computational Graph shows how data flows through a graph of basic operations (nodes)

title: Loss and Gradients
text:
Backpropagation






Chain Rule



\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} &= \left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial a}\right)\left(\frac{\partial a}{\partial z}\right) \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Loss \(L\)
Activations: \(a\)
Outputs: \(z\)
Weights (or biases): \(w\)



\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial a} &= -\frac{y}{a} + \frac{1-y}{1-a}\\
\frac{\partial a}{\partial z} &= a(1-a)~~~\mbox{for sigmoid}\\
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_j} &= x_j~~~; ~~~\frac{\partial z}{\partial b} = 1
\end{aligned}
\]






Backward Calculation




derivatives = lookup and multiplication
decompose larger calculation into smaller local parts
strict computational flow ensures that all required contributions are known
importance of differentiable activation functions: \(\frac{\partial a}{\partial z}\)
attaches a gradient to each forward value: \(a \to da \equiv \frac{\partial L}{\partial a}\)
flexibility: other loss functions, other inputs, more layers





Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def sigmoid(x):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 50)
y_sigmoid = sigmoid(x)
y_prime = y_sigmoid * ( 1 - y_sigmoid) 
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.plot(x, y_sigmoid, label='Sigmoid', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(x, y_prime, label='Derviative', linewidth=2)
plt.title("Sigmoid and Derivative")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

title: Loss and Gradients
text:
Summary: Forward and Backward Flows
The simple example (1 layer logistic regression) generalizes

to deeper neural network with multiple layers
parameters (weights and biases) at each layer \(l\)
activities \(a\) are calculated forward
gradients \(dL/da \equiv da\) are calculated backward




Forward and Backward Propagation through layer \(l\)








Important




gradients are byproduct of backpropagation
shapes of gradients agree with shape of tensors: \([\frac{\partial L}{\partial a}] = [da] = [a]\)
matrix notation for book-keeping/simplification and vectorized speedup
lookup and matrix multiplication

title: Overfitting with DNN
text:
Define Model and Training Loop
Here we define a relatively simple model class and a standardard training loop for convenience.
Notice: two parameters, \(\delta\) and \(\lambda\), to be used later.


Code
class Model1(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, delta=0.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.delta = delta
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(1, 16),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(delta),
            nn.Linear(16, 1),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

def model_train(model, X, y, epochs=1000, lr=0.05, lam=0.0):
    # standard training loop with MSE loss
    loss_func = torch.nn.MSELoss()
    opt = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)
    loss_hist = []
    model.train()
    for _ in range(epochs):
        opt.zero_grad()
        loss_reg = lam * sum(p.pow(2).sum() for p in model.parameters())
        loss = loss_func(model(X), y) + loss_reg
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()

        loss_hist.append(loss.detach().numpy())
    return loss_hist

title: Overfitting with DNN
text:
Loss History


Code
def plot_losses(losses):
    plt.figure()
    plt.plot(losses)
    plt.ylabel('loss')
    plt.xlabel('epoch')
    plt.title(f'Loss history. Final Loss: {losses[-1]:.4f}')
    plt.show()

plot_losses(loss_hist)

title: Overfitting with DNN
text:
Model Predictions


Code
def plot_pred(x,y, x_new, model, title=""):
    # plot training data and model predictions

    # no not use dropout during evaluation !
    model.eval() 
    with torch.no_grad():
        y_pred = model(x_new)

    plt.figure(figsize=(7,5))
    # training data
    plt.scatter(x, y, color='black', label='train data', s=50)
    # predictions
    plt.plot(x_new, y_pred.detach(), color='orange', lw=3, label=f"model (delta={model.delta:.2f})")
    plt.xlabel("x")
    plt.ylabel("y")
    plt.title(title)
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

x_plot = torch.linspace(-2.1, 2.1, 200).unsqueeze(1)
plot_pred(x,y, x_plot, model, title="Universality and Overfitting")

title: Overfitting with DNN
text:
With Dropout
… adjust model (during training).
This sets to zero the output of a random fraction (10-20%) of neurons for each mini-batch (epochs in the simple example below). In this way the model predictions should be robust against the presence or absence of individual neurons and overfit less.


Code
model = Model1(delta=0.1)
#print(summary(model))
loss_hist = model_train(model, x, y, epochs=10000, lr=1e-3)
plot_losses(loss_hist)
plot_pred(x,y, x_plot, model, title=f"delta={model.delta:.2f}")






















Dropout (\(delta>0\))




corresponds to ensemble of smaller networks within big one
makes learning harder
increases loss and loss fluctuations
reduces network capacity \(\to\) increased training time
forces redundant representations (c.f neurobiology: neurons are not reliable
makes model more robust \(\to\) less overfitting
accuracy \(\to\) reliability
only at training (pyTorch: model.eval at inference!)

or run multiple evaluations to estimate empirical probabilities

title: Overfitting with DNN
text:
With regularization
… adjust cost function \[
L(\theta) \to L(\theta) + \frac{\lambda}{2} \sum_l \theta_l^2
\]
This is also know as “L2 regularization” which encourages more balanced distribution of weights \(\theta_l\).
… and parameter update \[
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \fract{\partial L}{\partial \theta_t} - \lambda \theta_t
\]


Code

title: Frameworks
text:
Machine Learning and Deep-Learning have been powered by a number of open frameworks to simplify all modeling steps:
Here we review 3 popular frameworks: scikit-learn, pytorch, keras/tensorflow (not an exhaustive list)

title: Frameworks
text:
Goal: \(X \to Y = f(X)\)
Remember the simple example from the lecture.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.array([-1,  0, 1, 2, 3, 4])     # Data x
y = np.array([-3, -1, 1, 3, 5, 7])     # Answer y

plt.plot(x,y,'o')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('y')
plt.show()

title: Frameworks
text:
scikit-learn (2007++)

Pythonic ML-alternative to R
designed for small datasets (in-memory)
no GPU support



Code
import sklearn
from sklearn import linear_model
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
print('sklearn version:', sklearn.__version__)

lm = linear_model.LinearRegression()  # define model
xr = x.reshape(-1,1)                  # reshape data for tool (samples as rows) --> x[:, np.newaxis]
lm.fit(xr, y)                         # fit c.f. R: lm(Y ~ X)

# report fit
print(f"Fitted Parameters {lm.intercept_}, {lm.coef_}")

yp = lm.predict(xr)                   # prediction
MSE= mean_squared_error(y, yp)        # evaluate fit. other scores: R2=lm.score(xr, y)
print(f"Mean Squared Error: {MSE}")

# predict y for some new x
x_new=np.array([10, -40])
y_new = lm.predict(x_new.reshape(-1,1))

print(f"predictions:  {y_new}")

title: Frameworks
text:
PyTorch (Facebook 2017)

Pythonic alternative to Tensorflow 1.x
default choice in AI research and community
backed by Linux Foundation



Code
import torch
#import torch.nn as nn
#import torch.optim as optim
print('torch version:', torch.__version__)

# Convert data to PyTorch tensors 
# define dtype and reshape to column vectors (c.f np.reshape())
x_tensor = torch.tensor(x, dtype=torch.float32).view(-1, 1)
y_tensor = torch.tensor(y, dtype=torch.float32).view(-1, 1)

# Define Model
model = torch.nn.Sequential(
    torch.nn.Linear(1, 1)  # input dim = 1, output dim = 1
)

# Define Loss function
loss_function = torch.nn.MSELoss()
# Choose optimizer
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01)

# Fit model (explicit loop over epochs)
loss_history = []
model.train()             # put model in train mode 
for epoch in range(100):

    outputs = model(x_tensor)               # forward calculation
    loss = loss_function(outputs, y_tensor) # loss calculation
    loss_history.append(loss.item())        # append loss for tracking

    # Backward pass
    optimizer.zero_grad() # set .grad attribute in nn.Parameter to zero
    loss.backward()       # backpropagation: calculate gradients and store in .grad
    optimizer.step()      # update parameters: parm = parm - lr*parm.grad

# Report best fit - convert to torch tensor to numpy
for name, param in model.named_parameters():
    print(f"{name}: {param.data.numpy().flatten()}")

# report last (=best?) loss from history
print("Mean Squared Error (loss):", loss_history[-1])

# make predictions
x_new = np.array([10.0, -40.0])
x_new_tensor = torch.tensor(x_new, dtype=torch.float32).view(-1, 1)
y_new = model(x_new_tensor).detach().numpy()

y_ana = -1 + 2.0 * np.array(x_new)

print("analytical: ", y_ana)
print("numerical:  ", y_new.flatten())

.. _iris_dataset:

Iris plants dataset
--------------------

**Data Set Characteristics:**

:Number of Instances: 150 (50 in each of three classes)
:Number of Attributes: 4 numeric, predictive attributes and the class
:Attribute Information:
    - sepal length in cm
    - sepal width in cm
    - petal length in cm
    - petal width in cm
    - class:
            - Iris-Setosa
            - Iris-Versicolour
            - Iris-Virginica

:Summary Statistics:

============== ==== ==== ======= ===== ====================
                Min  Max   Mean    SD   Class Correlation
============== ==== ==== ======= ===== ====================
sepal length:   4.3  7.9   5.84   0.83    0.7826
sepal width:    2.0  4.4   3.05   0.43   -0.4194
petal length:   1.0  6.9   3.76   1.76    0.9490  (high!)
petal width:    0.1  2.5   1.20   0.76    0.9565  (high!)
============== ==== ==== ======= ===== ====================

:Missing Attribute Values: None
:Class Distribution: 33.3% for each of 3 classes.
:Creator: R.A. Fisher
:Donor: Michael Marshall (MARSHALL%PLU@io.arc.nasa.gov)
:Date: July, 1988

The famous Iris database, first used by Sir R.A. Fisher. The dataset is taken
from Fisher's paper. Note that it's the same as in R, but not as in the UCI
Machine Learning Repository, which has two wrong data points.

This is perhaps the best known database to be found in the
pattern recognition literature.  Fisher's paper is a classic in the field and
is referenced frequently to this day.  (See Duda & Hart, for example.)  The
data set contains 3 classes of 50 instances each, where each class refers to a
type of iris plant.  One class is linearly separable from the other 2; the
latter are NOT linearly separable from each other.

.. dropdown:: References

  - Fisher, R.A. "The use of multiple measurements in taxonomic problems"
    Annual Eugenics, 7, Part II, 179-188 (1936); also in "Contributions to
    Mathematical Statistics" (John Wiley, NY, 1950).
  - Duda, R.O., & Hart, P.E. (1973) Pattern Classification and Scene Analysis.
    (Q327.D83) John Wiley & Sons.  ISBN 0-471-22361-1.  See page 218.
  - Dasarathy, B.V. (1980) "Nosing Around the Neighborhood: A New System
    Structure and Classification Rule for Recognition in Partially Exposed
    Environments".  IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine
    Intelligence, Vol. PAMI-2, No. 1, 67-71.
  - Gates, G.W. (1972) "The Reduced Nearest Neighbor Rule".  IEEE Transactions
    on Information Theory, May 1972, 431-433.
  - See also: 1988 MLC Proceedings, 54-64.  Cheeseman et al"s AUTOCLASS II
    conceptual clustering system finds 3 classes in the data.
  - Many, many more ...



See also: Wikipedia

Histogram
A histogram shows the distribution of values.

One histogram over all values
This is easy to compute, but it mixes different variables with different scales and meanings. That makes it only of limited use.

Histograms per feature
A more informative plot shows one histogram per variable.


Code

sns.displot(
    data=df_long,
    x="Value",
    col="Feature",
    col_wrap=2,
    bins=15,
    facet_kws={"sharex": False, "sharey": False}
)
plt.show()


<Figure size 900x750 with 0 Axes>











Exercise 3
Why is the faceted version more informative than the single histogram over all values?


Hidden solution

The combined histogram mixes values from four different variables. That hides the individual distribution of each variable.
The faceted version lets us inspect:

center,
spread,
skewness,
possible multimodality

for each feature separately.

title: 01 Machine Learning
text:
Scale transformation
Scaling is often useful when variables live on different scales. Here we use standardization:
[ z = ]
After scaling:

each variable has mean approximately 0,
each variable has standard deviation approximately 1.

Important:

scaling changes the units and relative spread,
scaling does not change the ranking of observations within one variable,
scaling does not change Pearson correlations.



Code
scaler = StandardScaler()
Xs = scaler.fit_transform(X)

short_names = ["SL", "SW", "PL", "PW"]
df_scaled = pd.DataFrame(Xs, columns=short_names)
df_scaled["species"] = pd.Categorical.from_codes(y, species_names)

df_scaled_long = df_scaled.melt(
    id_vars="species",
    value_vars=short_names,
    var_name="Feature",
    value_name="Value"
)

print("Means after scaling")
print(df_scaled.drop(columns="species").mean())
print()
print("Standard deviations after scaling")
print(df_scaled.drop(columns="species").std())


Means after scaling

dtype: float64

Standard deviations after scaling

PL    1.00335

Before and after scaling


Code

sns.violinplot(data=df_long, x="Feature", y="Value", ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Before scaling")
axes[0].tick_params(axis="x", rotation=20)

sns.violinplot(data=df_scaled_long, x="Feature", y="Value", ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("After scaling")

plt.show()











Code
